name: b-loop-vs-range-bn
description: Tests whether the model uses b.Loop() (Go 1.24+) instead of the legacy for range b.N pattern
prompt: Write a Go benchmark for a function `ComputeHash(data []byte) [32]byte` that uses SHA-256. The project uses Go 1.24. Include setup code that loads a 1MB test fixture.
trap: Without the skill, the model defaults to `for i := 0; i < b.N; i++` or `for range b.N` which requires manual b.ResetTimer() and a sink variable to prevent dead code elimination

assertions.text: Uses b.Loop() as the benchmark loop construct
Setup code (loading fixture) is placed BEFORE the b.Loop() call, not inside it
Does NOT use b.ResetTimer() since b.Loop() automatically excludes setup
Does NOT use a package-level sink variable to prevent dead code elimination
Does NOT use `for i := 0; i < b.N; i++` or `for range b.N`

name: dead-code-elimination-awareness
description: Tests whether the model understands that b.Loop() prevents compiler dead code elimination
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest adding b.ResetTimer() or increasing benchtime, missing the core issue of dead code elimination

assertions.text: Identifies dead code elimination as the cause — the compiler optimizes away the ParseJSON call because its result is unused
Recommends migrating to b.Loop() as the primary fix since it prevents DCE automatically
If mentioning the legacy workaround, describes using a package-level sink variable (not a local one)
Explains that b.Loop() also automatically excludes the setup code from timing

name: count-flag-statistical-significance
description: Tests whether the model recommends -count=10 for statistical significance rather than single runs
prompt: I want to compare the performance of two JSON serialization approaches. How should I run the benchmarks to get reliable comparison data?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest a single `go test -bench=.` run per version without -count, making benchstat comparison impossible

assertions.text: Recommends -count=10 (or higher) for statistical significance
Recommends using benchstat to compare the two runs
Recommends -benchmem to track allocation metrics
Recommends saving output to files (e.g., via tee) for later benchstat comparison
Uses -run='^$' to skip unit tests during benchmark runs

name: benchstat-output-interpretation
description: Tests understanding of benchstat output format including p-value, tilde symbol, and confidence intervals
prompt:
I ran benchstat and got this output. What does it mean? Should I merge my optimization?

```
          │   old.txt   │              new.txt               │
          │   sec/op    │   sec/op     vs base               │
Parse-16    4.592µ ± 8%   4.481µ ± 7%  ~ (p=0.089 n=10)
```
trap: Without the skill, the model may interpret the lower number as an improvement and recommend merging

assertions.text: Explains that the ~ symbol means no statistically significant difference was detected
States that p=0.089 is above the 0.05 significance threshold
Notes that the wide confidence intervals (±8%, ±7%) overlap, making the result untrustworthy
Advises NOT to claim improvement based on this result
Suggests increasing -count to 20+ or reducing noise sources as next steps

name: p-hacking-awareness
description: Tests whether the model warns against rerunning benchmarks until significance appears
prompt: My benchstat comparison keeps showing `~` (no significant difference). Can I just rerun the benchmarks a few more times and pick the run that shows significance?
trap: Without the skill, the model may naively agree or suggest cherry-picking favorable runs

assertions.text: Explicitly warns against 'retry until significant' as selection bias / p-hacking
Explains that rerunning until ~ disappears introduces bias
Recommends increasing -count ONCE and accepting the result
Mentions that at alpha=0.05, ~5% of benchmarks will randomly show significance (false positives)
Suggests the change may genuinely have no measurable effect

name: interleaving-benchmark-runs
description: Tests knowledge of interleaving old/new benchmark runs to reduce systematic bias
prompt: I'm comparing performance before and after an optimization. I ran all 10 baseline iterations first, then all 10 optimized iterations. My colleague says this approach is flawed. Why?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not know about systematic bias from sequential runs or may not suggest pre-compilation with go test -c

assertions.text: Identifies systematic bias: thermal throttling, background processes, CPU frequency scaling can differ between the first batch and second batch
Recommends interleaving runs (alternating old/new) to reduce this bias
Recommends pre-compiling both versions with `go test -c` to avoid measuring compilation time
Shows running the pre-compiled test binaries directly (e.g., ./old.test -test.bench=...)
Explains that without pre-compilation, each go test -bench invocation includes compilation overhead that varies

name: alloc-objects-vs-inuse-space
description: Tests understanding of when to use alloc_objects vs inuse_space heap profile types
prompt: My Go service has high GC CPU overhead (30% of CPU in runtime.mallocgc according to pprof). I captured a heap profile. Should I look at alloc_objects, alloc_space, or inuse_space?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest inuse_space (which shows live objects, not allocation rate) or alloc_space (which shows bytes, not count)

assertions.text: Recommends alloc_objects as the primary choice for GC pressure / high allocation rate
Explains that GC cares about object count, not size — alloc_objects counts allocation events
Explains that inuse_space shows currently live objects and is for leak detection, not GC churn
Distinguishes alloc_space (total bytes allocated) as useful for reducing peak memory, not GC frequency
Mentions that runtime.mallocgc dominating CPU profile indicates allocation rate is the bottleneck, not computation

name: pprof-flat-vs-cum
description: Tests understanding of flat vs cumulative time in pprof and when to use top -cum
prompt: I ran `go tool pprof cpu.prof` and the top command shows runtime.mallocgc, runtime.memmove, and runtime.scanobject as the top functions. These are all runtime functions I can't modify. How do I find which of MY functions is causing this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest trying to optimize runtime functions or be unsure how to trace back to application code

assertions.text: Recommends using `top -cum` to find application functions with high cumulative time
Explains that runtime functions appearing in top (flat) are symptoms, not causes — they're called by application code
Explains the difference: flat = time in the function itself, cum = time in the function + everything it calls
Suggests using `list` or `peek` to drill into the application functions that delegate to these runtime calls
Explains the 'flat low + cum high' pattern: the function is a coordinator calling expensive things

name: escape-analysis-interpretation
description: Tests ability to use and interpret escape analysis output
prompt: My benchmark shows unexpected heap allocations for a function that only uses local variables. How can I find out why Go is allocating on the heap instead of the stack?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest generic profiling without mentioning the specific compiler flag for escape analysis

assertions.text: Recommends `go build -gcflags="-m"` to show escape decisions
Mentions `-m -m` (double -m) for verbose output showing the escape chain / reason
Lists common escape causes like returning pointer to local, interface boxing, closure captures
Notes that this analysis is free (compile-time, no runtime overhead)
Advises to only investigate escapes in hot functions identified by pprof, not all functions

name: inlining-budget-and-blockers
description: Tests knowledge of Go's inlining cost budget and common blockers
prompt: I have a small helper function in a hot path that I want the Go compiler to inline. How do I check if it's being inlined, and what might prevent it?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not know the specific budget number (80) or all the blockers like defer, recover, go statements

assertions.text: Recommends `go build -gcflags="-m"` and grepping for 'can inline' or 'cannot inline'
Mentions the inline cost budget of 80 (as of Go 1.22+)
Lists defer as an inlining blocker
Lists recover() as an inlining blocker
Mentions that splitting large functions into smaller ones can help the hot inner function inline

name: trace-vs-pprof-selection
description: Tests judgment on when to use execution trace vs pprof
prompt: My Go HTTP service has high P99 latency (500ms) but pprof CPU profile shows only 15% CPU utilization. The top functions in the CPU profile are all fast. Where is the time going?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest more CPU profiling or generic optimization, missing that this is a scheduling/blocking problem requiring the execution tracer

assertions.text: Identifies this as a case where pprof is insufficient — low CPU with high latency means goroutines are waiting, not working
Recommends `go tool trace` (execution tracer) to see scheduling delays and blocking
Explains that pprof only shows on-CPU time; trace shows off-CPU waiting states
Suggests looking at goroutine states: yellow/orange (runnable but waiting for P) or red/pink (blocked on I/O, channel, mutex)
Mentions the -pprof=sync or -pprof=net flag to extract blocking profiles from trace data

name: benchstat-unit-normalization
description: Tests understanding of benchstat's automatic unit normalization
prompt: I'm confused by benchstat output. My benchmark reports `ns/op` but benchstat shows `sec/op` with a µ prefix. Is this an error?
trap: Without the skill, the model may think there's a bug or unit mismatch

assertions.text: Explains that benchstat automatically normalizes units for display
States that ns/op is displayed as sec/op with µ (micro) prefix to avoid nonsensical 'µns/op'
Mentions that MB/s is similarly normalized to B/s with K, M, G prefixes
Confirms this is expected behavior, not an error

name: benchstat-filter-syntax
description: Tests knowledge of benchstat's filter expression syntax for selecting specific benchmarks
prompt: I have benchmark output with many sub-benchmarks like BenchmarkParse/format=json, BenchmarkParse/format=gob, BenchmarkEncode/size=1k, etc. I only want to compare the json format benchmarks. How do I filter benchstat output?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest grepping the output file instead of using benchstat's built-in filter syntax

assertions.text: Uses benchstat's -filter flag rather than pre-processing with grep
Shows the correct filter syntax: -filter '/format:json' or similar key:value pattern
Mentions regex support in filters (e.g., .name:/Parse/)
Mentions logical operators (AND, OR, negation with -) in filter expressions

name: benchstat-projection-col-flag
description: Tests knowledge of benchstat's -col flag for comparing sub-benchmark parameters
prompt: I have a single benchmark file with results for BenchmarkEncode/format=json and BenchmarkEncode/format=gob. I want to compare json vs gob performance side by side in benchstat. How?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest splitting into two files and comparing, or not know about the -col flag

assertions.text: Uses the -col /format flag to create columns from sub-benchmark parameter values
Shows the correct command: benchstat -col /format bench.txt
Mentions -row .name to simplify row names by stripping sub-benchmark config
Mentions the @() sort modifier for controlling column order (e.g., /format@(gob json))

name: benchstat-assume-exact
description: Tests knowledge of benchstat's assume=exact unit metadata for non-varying metrics
prompt: I want to track binary size across commits using benchstat. The size doesn't vary between runs (it's deterministic). But benchstat complains about insufficient samples when I use -count=1. How do I handle deterministic metrics?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest using -count=10 anyway (wasteful for deterministic metrics) or abandoning benchstat

assertions.text: Recommends the assume=exact unit metadata annotation
Shows the syntax: Unit <metric> assume=exact in benchmark output
Explains that assume=exact disables non-parametric statistics
Notes that benchstat will warn if values vary when assume=exact is set
States that single measurement (no -count needed) works with assume=exact

name: ci-regression-tool-selection
description: Tests judgment on which CI regression detection tool to use
prompt:
I want to add automated benchmark regression detection to my CI pipeline. I need something that:
1. Compares PR performance against the base branch
2. Uses statistical analysis (not just single-run comparison)
3. Integrates with our GitHub Actions workflow

What tool should I use?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest writing a custom script or using only raw benchstat without knowing about benchdiff

assertions.text: Recommends benchdiff as the primary tool for PR-to-base comparison with statistical rigor
Explains that benchdiff uses benchstat internally for statistical analysis
Mentions cob as a simpler alternative but notes it uses single-run comparison without benchstat-style statistics
Mentions gobenchdata for long-term trend tracking and visualization
Explains the tradeoff: benchdiff=high rigor, cob=quick+simple, gobenchdata=trends+dashboard

name: cob-data-loss-warning
description: Tests awareness of cob's destructive behavior with uncommitted changes
prompt: I want to use `cob` for quick benchmark regression checking in my local development workflow. Any caveats?
trap: Without the skill, the model may recommend cob for local use without the critical safety warning

assertions.text: Warns that cob uses `git reset` internally which can cause data loss with uncommitted changes
Recommends committing all work before running cob
Suggests running cob only in CI pipelines, not locally
Notes that cob compares single runs without benchstat-style statistics, making it susceptible to noise
Mentions [skip cob] commit message convention to bypass checks

name: noisy-neighbor-mitigation
description: Tests knowledge of why CI benchmarks are noisy and mitigation strategies
prompt: Our CI benchmark results fluctuate wildly — sometimes showing 15% regression, sometimes 10% improvement, for the same code. We're using GitHub-hosted runners. How do we fix this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest tightening thresholds (which causes more false positives) or simply retrying

assertions.text: Explains that shared CI runners have 5-10% variance due to noisy neighbors
Recommends running both base and head benchmarks in the same CI job for relative comparison
Recommends using -count=10+ with benchstat to filter noise statistically
Suggests conservative thresholds (20%+) on shared runners rather than tight thresholds
Warns against 'retry until pass' as selection bias
Mentions dedicated/self-hosted runners as the definitive solution for critical benchmarks

name: self-hosted-runner-tuning
description: Tests knowledge of system-level tuning for reproducible benchmarks on self-hosted runners
prompt: We have a dedicated self-hosted CI runner for benchmark tests. What system-level settings should we configure to minimize benchmark variance?
trap: Without the skill, the model may only suggest generic OS tuning without knowing the specific settings for benchmark stability

assertions.text: Recommends disabling CPU frequency scaling by setting the 'performance' governor
Recommends disabling Turbo Boost (Intel no_turbo or AMD boost)
Recommends pinning benchmarks to specific CPU cores using taskset
Recommends disabling SMT/Hyper-Threading to avoid execution unit sharing
Warns that these settings should ONLY be applied to dedicated runners, never developer machines

name: taskset-core-pinning-rationale
description: Tests understanding of WHY core pinning helps benchmarks
prompt: Why does pinning a Go benchmark to specific CPU cores with taskset help reduce variance? What's the underlying mechanism?
trap: Without the skill, the model may give a vague answer about CPU contention without explaining the cache thrashing mechanism

assertions.text: Explains that without pinning, the OS migrates the process across cores
Explains that L1/L2 caches are per-core, so migration causes cache thrashing
Recommends leaving cores 0-1 for OS and other processes, using cores 2+ for benchmarks
Shows the taskset command syntax (e.g., taskset -c 2,3 go test ...)

name: b-report-metric-custom
description: Tests knowledge of b.ReportMetric and b.Elapsed for custom benchmark metrics
prompt: I want my Go benchmark to report throughput in bytes/second in addition to the standard ns/op. How do I add custom metrics to benchmark output?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest manual calculation and fmt.Printf instead of the built-in b.ReportMetric

assertions.text: Uses b.ReportMetric() to add custom metrics
Uses b.Elapsed() to get the total benchmark duration
Shows the correct pattern: b.ReportMetric(float64(bytes)/b.Elapsed().Seconds(), "bytes/s")
The custom metric integrates with standard benchmark output format (not separate print statements)

name: memory-leak-detection-with-base
description: Tests the workflow for detecting memory leaks using pprof -base comparison
prompt: I suspect my Go HTTP service has a memory leak — RSS keeps growing over hours. How do I identify what's leaking?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest only looking at a single heap snapshot instead of comparing two snapshots over time

assertions.text: Recommends taking two heap snapshots separated by time (minutes to hours)
Uses pprof -base to diff the two snapshots, showing only what grew
Specifically recommends -inuse_space profile type for leak detection (not alloc_space)
Explains that alloc_space is cumulative since program start and includes freed objects
Mentions common leak causes: unbounded caches, maps that never shrink, goroutine leaks holding references

name: mutex-block-profile-enablement
description: Tests awareness that mutex and block profiles must be explicitly enabled
prompt: I want to profile mutex contention in my Go service. I fetched the mutex profile from /debug/pprof/mutex but it's empty. What's wrong?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest the endpoint is broken or suggest different debugging approaches

assertions.text: Identifies that mutex profiling is disabled by default and must be explicitly enabled
Shows runtime.SetMutexProfileFraction() as the enablement call
Explains the fraction parameter (e.g., 5 means 1 out of 5 events recorded)
Recommends disabling after investigation (SetMutexProfileFraction(0)) to eliminate overhead
Mentions runtime.SetBlockProfileRate() for the related block profile

name: trace-custom-annotations
description: Tests knowledge of runtime/trace custom annotations (tasks, regions, logs)
prompt: I'm using go tool trace to analyze my HTTP handler but the trace timeline only shows generic goroutine activity. How can I add application-level context to see which request phases (validation, database query, serialization) are taking time?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest using log statements or pprof labels instead of trace-specific annotations

assertions.text: Recommends trace.NewTask for logical operations that may span goroutines
Recommends trace.WithRegion for phases within a task or goroutine
Mentions trace.Log for point-in-time markers in the trace
Shows correct usage with context propagation (ctx parameter)
Notes that annotations add negligible overhead when tracing is disabled

name: trace-gc-phase-interpretation
description: Tests ability to interpret GC phases in execution traces
prompt: I'm looking at an execution trace and I see large blocks of time where my goroutines show as 'GC assist' (blue). What does this mean and how do I fix it?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not explain the proportional allocation tax mechanism or the specific remediation

assertions.text: Explains that GC mark assist means goroutines are being drafted by the GC to help scan the heap
Explains that the runtime forces goroutines to assist in proportion to their allocation rate — heavy allocators get taxed more
Identifies this as a symptom of too many allocations, not a GC configuration problem
Recommends reducing allocation rate (the root cause) rather than tuning GOGC
Distinguishes mark assist from STW (stop-the-world) phases which affect all goroutines equally

name: trace-pprof-extraction
description: Tests knowledge of extracting pprof profiles from trace data
prompt: I have an execution trace file from a production service. I want to find which functions are responsible for the most network blocking time. Can I use the trace for this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest capturing a separate pprof profile instead of extracting from the trace

assertions.text: Uses `go tool trace -pprof=net trace.out > net.prof` to extract a network blocking profile
Then uses go tool pprof on the extracted profile for analysis (top, list, etc.)
Mentions other extractable profile types: sync, syscall, sched
Explains this bridges trace data (nanosecond events) with pprof analysis (statistical aggregation)

name: fieldalignment-no-autofix
description: Tests that the model uses fieldalignment without the -fix flag
prompt: I suspect some of my Go structs have suboptimal field ordering causing padding waste. How do I check?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest using fieldalignment with -fix flag or structlayout without the diagnostic-first approach

assertions.text: Recommends running `fieldalignment ./...` to detect padding waste
Does NOT use the -fix flag — the skill explicitly says to let the agent apply changes manually
Mentions using unsafe.Sizeof/Alignof/Offsetof to inspect struct layout before and after
Treats this as a diagnostic step, not an automatic fix

name: godebug-gctrace-runtime-diagnostics
description: Tests knowledge of GODEBUG environment variables for runtime diagnostics
prompt: I want to understand my Go service's GC behavior without adding any code or recompiling. Is there a way to get GC diagnostics at runtime?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest adding runtime.ReadMemStats calls or pprof endpoints requiring code changes

assertions.text: Recommends GODEBUG=gctrace=1 to get GC cycle information without recompiling
Describes what gctrace shows: GC frequency, pause times, heap sizes, CPU%
Mentions other GODEBUG options like schedtrace for scheduler diagnostics
Notes that these are configured via environment variables — no recompile needed

name: runtime-scanobject-cpu-diagnosis
description: Tests interpretation of runtime.scanobject appearing high in CPU profile
prompt: My CPU profile shows `runtime.scanobject` consuming 25% of CPU time. What does this mean and how do I reduce it?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not recognize this as a GC pointer scanning issue or may suggest wrong remediation

assertions.text: Identifies runtime.scanobject as GC pointer scanning — the GC is tracing pointers in the heap
Explains that the heap contains many pointers that the GC must trace
Recommends reducing pointer density: value types instead of pointers in slices/maps
Suggests flattening nested structures or using [N]byte arrays instead of strings in hot structs
References the golang-performance skill for optimization patterns

name: runtime-memmove-diagnosis
description: Tests interpretation of runtime.memmove appearing high in CPU profile
prompt: Pprof shows `runtime.memmove` taking 20% of CPU. What's causing this and how should I investigate?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest generic optimization without identifying the specific causes

assertions.text: Identifies runtime.memmove as large memory copies
Lists common causes: slice append growing beyond capacity, copy() of large slices, string-to-byte conversions
Recommends pre-allocating slices to final capacity
Suggests reusing buffers or working with []byte directly
Recommends using top -cum to find the application functions that trigger these memmoves

name: fgprof-off-cpu-profiling
description: Tests knowledge of fgprof for capturing off-CPU time that pprof misses
prompt: My Go service has high latency but the CPU profile barely shows any CPU usage. Standard pprof doesn't reveal where time is spent. What tool captures both on-CPU and off-CPU time in a single profile?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest only the execution tracer or not know about fgprof

assertions.text: Recommends fgprof (github.com/felixge/fgprof) for full goroutine profiling
Explains that fgprof captures both on-CPU and off-CPU (I/O wait) time in a single profile
Explains that standard pprof CPU profiles only show on-CPU time, missing I/O waits
Describes the use case: pprof shows low CPU% but latency is high

name: flight-recorder-go125
description: Tests knowledge of the Go 1.25 flight recorder for retroactive trace capture
prompt: My Go service occasionally experiences timeout spikes but I can't reproduce them. By the time I notice and start tracing, the problem is gone. Is there a way to capture trace data retroactively for these intermittent issues?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest continuous tracing (too expensive) or instrumented logging

assertions.text: Recommends the Go 1.25 flight recorder (trace.NewFlightRecorder)
Explains it keeps a circular buffer of recent trace data in memory
Shows snapshotting with WriteTo when the anomaly is detected
Mentions the MinAge and MaxBytes configuration parameters
Shows a trigger pattern (e.g., slow request detection with time.Since threshold)
Notes the constraint: at most one flight recorder active at a time

name: flight-recorder-sync-once-pattern
description: Tests the sync.Once pattern for flight recorder snapshots
prompt: I'm setting up a flight recorder in my Go service. I want to snapshot it when a slow request is detected, but multiple goroutines might detect slow requests simultaneously. How do I prevent multiple overlapping snapshots?
trap: Without the skill, the model may use a mutex or channel instead of the idiomatic sync.Once pattern

assertions.text: Uses sync.Once to ensure only one snapshot is taken
Calls fr.WriteTo inside the sync.Once.Do function
Notes that only one goroutine may call WriteTo at a time
Shows calling the snapshot in a separate goroutine (go captureSnapshot)
Shows fr.Stop() after WriteTo completes

name: flight-recorder-sizing
description: Tests understanding of flight recorder buffer sizing
prompt: I'm configuring a flight recorder for my Go service. My typical investigation window when a timeout occurs is about 5 seconds. How should I size the MinAge and MaxBytes parameters?
trap: Without the skill, the model may guess arbitrary values without the 2x rule or data rate context

assertions.text: Sets MinAge to ~2x the problem window (10 seconds for a 5-second investigation window)
Mentions that busy services generate ~1-10 MB/s of trace data
Recommends starting MaxBytes at 1-5 MiB and adjusting
Explains that MaxBytes takes precedence over MinAge — when buffer fills, older data is discarded

name: trace-timeline-color-coding
description: Tests ability to interpret execution trace timeline colors
prompt: I opened an execution trace in the web UI. I see lots of yellow/orange gaps before green segments on my goroutine lanes, and some red bands spanning all processor lanes. What does this indicate?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not know the specific color coding of the trace viewer

assertions.text: Identifies yellow/orange as runnable state — goroutines ready to run but waiting for a processor
Identifies red bands across all P lanes as GC stop-the-world pauses
Diagnoses the yellow gaps as CPU saturation — too many runnable goroutines competing for processors
Identifies green as actively executing/running state
Suggests examining goroutine count vs GOMAXPROCS to verify CPU saturation

name: pprof-web-ui-flamegraph
description: Tests knowledge of the pprof web UI as an alternative to CLI
prompt: I have a CPU profile and the text-based `top` output is hard to navigate for an unfamiliar codebase. Is there a more visual way to explore pprof profiles?
trap: Without the skill, the model may only suggest the SVG graph output

assertions.text: Recommends `go tool pprof -http=:8080 cpu.prof` to open the interactive web UI
Mentions the flamegraph view as the most intuitive visualization
Lists other web UI views: Graph, Top, Source, Disassembly, Peek
Notes that filters can be pre-applied with -focus flag when opening the web UI

name: pprof-focus-ignore-difference
description: Tests understanding of the difference between pprof's focus, ignore, show, and hide filters
prompt: In pprof, what's the difference between `focus`, `ignore`, `show`, and `hide`? When would I use each one?
trap: Without the skill, the model may confuse the cost accounting behavior of these filters

assertions.text: Explains that focus keeps only paths containing a matching function — everything else dropped
Explains that ignore removes matching functions entirely, attributing their costs to callers
Explains that show is like focus but only affects display, not cost accounting
Explains that hide is like ignore but only hides from display, not cost accounting
Mentions `reset` to clear all filters

name: pprof-tags-and-labels
description: Tests knowledge of pprof custom labels via pprof.Do() for multi-tenant profiling
prompt: I want to break down my CPU profile by request type (API vs batch). How can I add custom labels to pprof samples so I can filter and group by request type?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest separate profiles per request type instead of using pprof labels

assertions.text: Uses pprof.Labels() and pprof.Do() to add custom labels to profiled code
Shows the correct pattern: pprof.Do(ctx, pprof.Labels("key", "value"), func(ctx context.Context) {...})
Shows using tagfocus to filter by label (e.g., -tagfocus=request_type=api)
Shows using tagroot to group by label (e.g., -tagroot=request_type)
Mentions the tags command to see all tag keys and distributions

name: pprof-sample-index-switching
description: Tests knowledge of switching between metric types in a heap profile without reloading
prompt: I opened a heap profile in pprof interactive mode and I'm looking at alloc_objects. Now I want to also see inuse_space. Do I need to exit and reopen with different flags?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest exiting and reopening with a different flag

assertions.text: Uses sample_index command to switch metrics without reloading
Shows the correct syntax: sample_index=inuse_space
Lists available indices: alloc_objects, alloc_space, inuse_objects, inuse_space
Explains that heap profiles contain multiple metrics and you can switch between them interactively

name: pprof-granularity-lines
description: Tests knowledge of pprof granularity control for multi-hot-spot functions
prompt: I used `list` on a function and it shows two expensive lines, but the `top` output only shows the function name once with aggregated cost. How can I see per-line costs in the top output?
trap: Without the skill, the model may only suggest using list for per-line analysis

assertions.text: Uses granularity=lines to group by exact source line in top output
Mentions other granularity levels: functions (default), filefunctions, files, addresses
Shows the command: either `granularity=lines` in interactive mode or `-granularity=lines` as flag

name: ssa-dump-investigation
description: Tests knowledge of SSA dump for understanding compiler optimization passes
prompt: I want to understand exactly what optimizations the Go compiler applies to a specific function. I need more detail than escape analysis or inlining flags provide. Is there a way to see the compiler's intermediate representation?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not know about the GOSSAFUNC environment variable

assertions.text: Uses GOSSAFUNC=FunctionName go build to generate SSA dump
Mentions that it creates ssa.html which can be opened in a browser
Describes the optimization passes visible: source, AST, start SSA, optimization, lower, regalloc, genssa
Mentions what to look for: remaining bounds checks, dead code elimination, constant folding, register spills
Mentions clicking on values to highlight them across passes

name: assembly-output-heap-allocation-detection
description: Tests using assembly output to verify heap allocations in hot paths
prompt: I optimized a function to avoid heap allocations but want to verify at the assembly level that no calls to runtime.makeslice or runtime.newobject remain. How?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest only escape analysis instead of the more definitive assembly inspection

assertions.text: Uses `go build -gcflags="-S"` to generate assembly output
Shows grepping for CALL runtime.makeslice, runtime.newobject, or runtime.growslice
Mentions `go tool objdump` as an alternative for disassembling compiled binaries
Mentions `-S` flag for source-interleaved disassembly

name: value-receiver-inlining-advantage
description: Tests knowledge that value receivers enable better inlining than pointer receivers
prompt: I'm designing a fluent API for a small config builder in a performance-sensitive path. Should I use value receivers or pointer receivers for the method chain? Consider both correctness and compiler optimization.
trap: Without the skill, the model defaults to pointer receivers for method chains without considering inlining implications

assertions.text: Mentions that value receivers enable full inlining of method chains
Explains that pointer receivers add indirection that can block inlining for fluent APIs
Recommends checking with -gcflags="-m" to verify inlining behavior
Considers both the performance trade-off and the struct size (value receivers copy the struct)

name: prometheus-go-metrics-vs-runtime-metrics
description: Tests understanding that runtime/metrics are NOT the same as Prometheus metrics
prompt: I want to monitor Go runtime metrics in my Prometheus dashboard. Can I just use Go's runtime/metrics package directly in my PromQL queries?
trap: Without the skill, the model may conflate runtime/metrics keys with Prometheus metric names

assertions.text: Clarifies that runtime/metrics are Go internal data structures, not Prometheus metrics
Explains that prometheus/client_golang selectively converts some runtime/metrics into Prometheus format
Lists the actual Prometheus metric names (e.g., go_memstats_alloc_bytes, go_goroutines)
Mentions that by default only traditional go_memstats_* and go_gc_* are exposed

name: go-memstats-stw-overhead
description: Tests awareness of ReadMemStats causing stop-the-world pauses
prompt: I'm using prometheus/client_golang to expose Go runtime metrics. My high-throughput service occasionally shows brief latency spikes correlated with Prometheus scrape intervals. Could the metrics collection itself be causing this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not connect metrics collection to STW pauses

assertions.text: Identifies that go_memstats_* metrics internally call runtime.ReadMemStats() which triggers a short stop-the-world pause
Recommends the Go 1.17+ runtime/metrics-based collector as lower overhead alternative
Shows the code to register the modern collector: collectors.NewGoCollector with GoRuntimeMetricsCollection
Recommends using a custom prometheus.NewRegistry() rather than the default one

name: promql-gc-pressure-queries
description: Tests knowledge of specific PromQL queries for GC pressure investigation
prompt: I suspect my Go service has excessive GC pressure. What Prometheus queries should I use to confirm this and find the root cause?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest generic monitoring queries without the specific GC-related PromQL

assertions.text: Uses rate(go_gc_duration_seconds_count[5m]) for GC frequency (cycles per second)
States that >2 GC cycles/s sustained indicates excessive allocation rate
Uses go_gc_duration_seconds{quantile="1"} for worst-case GC pause
Uses rate(go_memstats_alloc_bytes_total[5m]) for allocation rate comparison before/after deploy
Recommends correlating with P99 latency to confirm GC pauses cause tail latency

name: promql-goroutine-leak-detection
description: Tests specific PromQL patterns for detecting goroutine leaks
prompt: How do I detect goroutine leaks in my production Go service using Prometheus metrics?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest only looking at the absolute go_goroutines count without the delta pattern

assertions.text: Uses go_goroutines gauge for current count
Uses delta(go_goroutines[1h]) for net goroutine change — positive without load increase indicates leak
Notes that goroutine count should correlate with load — independent growth is a leak signal
Suggests an alerting rule with threshold (e.g., go_goroutines > 10000)

name: investigation-session-setup
description: Tests the structured approach to production performance investigation sessions
prompt: I need to do a deep-dive performance investigation on one instance of my Go service in production. What should I set up before I start collecting profiles?
trap: Without the skill, the model may jump straight to pprof without the investigation session preparation

assertions.text: Recommends reducing Prometheus scrape interval to <=10s on the target instance
Recommends enabling pprof via environment variable without recompile
Recommends enabling continuous profiling on only the target instance, not fleet-wide
Emphasizes reverting all changes after investigation
Mentions the key design principle: all debug features should be toggleable via environment variables

name: cost-warnings-trace-duration
description: Tests awareness of the cost and practical limits of execution traces
prompt: I want to capture a 5-minute execution trace of my Go service to analyze a periodic issue that happens every 2-3 minutes. Is this feasible?
trap: Without the skill, the model may agree to a 5-minute trace without warning about data volume

assertions.text: Warns that traces generate data at MB/s — a 5-minute trace would be enormous (potentially GBs)
Recommends keeping traces to 5-10 seconds maximum
Warns that large traces are slow to parse and may need 1GB+ RAM to open
Suggests using the flight recorder (Go 1.25+) as an alternative for intermittent issues
Notes that the browser UI struggles with traces >100MB

name: benchstat-three-version-comparison
description: Tests knowledge of comparing more than two versions with benchstat
prompt: I have benchmark results from three different versions of my code (v1, v2, v3). I want to compare all three in a single benchstat output. How?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest running benchstat twice for pairwise comparisons

assertions.text: Shows labeling inputs: benchstat v1=v1.txt v2=v2.txt v3=v3.txt
Explains that the first input is always the base for comparison
States that v2 vs v1 and v3 vs v1 comparisons are shown (both relative to first input)

name: host-level-correlation
description: Tests awareness of correlating Go metrics with host-level metrics
prompt: My Go service shows high process_cpu_seconds_total but the CPU profile looks normal. Could the problem be outside my application?
trap: Without the skill, the model may only investigate within the Go application

assertions.text: Recommends checking node_exporter metrics for host-level CPU, memory, disk I/O
Explains the noisy neighbor pattern: high node_cpu with low process_cpu = external contention
Mentions process-exporter for per-process metrics when multiple services share a host
Suggests correlating Go app metrics with infrastructure metrics to determine if the problem is in the app or the environment

name: pprof-diff-base-vs-base
description: Tests understanding of -base vs -diff_base flags in pprof for comparison
prompt: I want to compare two CPU profiles — one before and one after my optimization. What's the difference between `pprof -base` and `pprof -diff_base`?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not know both flags exist or may confuse their semantics

assertions.text: Explains that -base subtracts base from source — all values become deltas
Explains that -diff_base shows percentages relative to the base profile
Mentions -normalize flag for making ratios comparable when capture durations differ
Shows how to generate a diff SVG for visual comparison

name: gobenchdata-github-action-setup
description: Tests knowledge of setting up gobenchdata for long-term trend tracking
prompt: I want to track benchmark performance trends over time with an interactive web dashboard. I'm using GitHub Actions. What's the best approach?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest building a custom dashboard or only using benchstat

assertions.text: Recommends gobenchdata for trend tracking and visualization
Shows the GitHub Action configuration with bobheadxi/gobenchdata@v1
Mentions publishing to gh-pages for the dashboard
Shows the regression checks config (.gobenchdata-checks.yml) with thresholds
Mentions PRUNE_COUNT to limit stored history

name: benchstat-single-file-summary
description: Tests knowledge of using benchstat with a single file for variance analysis
prompt: I haven't made any code changes yet. I just want to check if my benchmarks are stable (low variance) before I start optimizing. Can benchstat help?
trap: Without the skill, the model may say benchstat requires two files for comparison

assertions.text: Shows running benchstat with a single file: benchstat bench.txt
Explains it shows median and confidence interval for each benchmark
Recommends using this to check measurement stability before making changes
Notes that high variance (± >5%) indicates noisy benchmarks needing more runs or better isolation

name: count-minimum-by-scenario
description: Tests knowledge of appropriate -count values for different scenarios
prompt: How many benchmark iterations (-count) should I use? I've seen recommendations ranging from 1 to 30.
trap: Without the skill, the model may give a single number without context-dependent guidance

assertions.text: Recommends 6 minimum for quick local checks
Recommends 10 for standard pre-merge comparisons
Recommends 20-30 for detecting small changes (<5%)
Recommends 20+ for noisy CI environments
Explicitly warns against -count=1 as providing no variance information

name: interface-boxing-escape
description: Tests understanding of interface boxing as a common escape cause
prompt: My escape analysis shows variables escaping to heap at a line where I'm passing a struct to a function that takes an `any` parameter. Why does this cause a heap allocation?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not explain the boxing mechanism clearly

assertions.text: Explains interface boxing: storing a concrete type in interface{}/any allocates a copy on the heap
Lists fmt.Sprintf as a common source of interface boxing (arguments boxed into any)
Suggests using concrete types instead of interfaces in hot paths to avoid boxing
Notes that this is especially impactful in functions called frequently (hot paths)

name: trace-scheduling-latency-diagnosis
description: Tests ability to diagnose scheduling latency from trace data
prompt: My execution trace shows many goroutines spending significant time in the 'runnable' (yellow) state before becoming 'running' (green). What does this mean and how do I fix it?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not connect runnable-to-running delay with CPU saturation

assertions.text: Identifies this as high scheduling latency — goroutines ready to run but waiting for a processor
Diagnoses the cause: too many goroutines competing for GOMAXPROCS processors (CPU saturation)
Suggests extracting the scheduling latency profile: go tool trace -pprof=sched trace.out
Recommends checking for uneven distribution across Ps (work imbalance)
Lists other potential causes: OS scheduling interference, goroutines pinned by cgo or long syscalls

name: benchmark-output-format-parsing
description: Tests understanding of the benchmark output format
prompt: I see this benchmark output: `BenchmarkEncode/size=64-8  5000000  230.5 ns/op  128 B/op  2 allocs/op`. What does each part mean?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not explain the -8 suffix correctly

assertions.text: Explains that -8 is the GOMAXPROCS suffix
Explains that 5000000 is the number of iterations (b.N)
Explains that ns/op is time per operation
Explains that B/op is bytes allocated per operation
Explains that allocs/op is heap allocation count per operation

name: pprof-show-from-framework-noise
description: Tests knowledge of show_from to trim framework noise in profiles
prompt: My pprof output is cluttered with HTTP framework routing functions (net/http.(*ServeMux).ServeHTTP, etc.) that appear above every handler. How do I remove this noise and start the analysis from my handler code?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest using ignore (which changes cost accounting) instead of show_from

assertions.text: Uses show_from=regex to trim all frames above the first matching function
Shows the correct pattern: show_from=handler.Handle or similar
Explains that show_from hides all callers above the match point
Differentiates from ignore which removes functions and re-attributes their costs

name: optional-prometheus-metrics-enablement
description: Tests knowledge of how to enable optional Go runtime Prometheus metrics
prompt: I want to expose Go scheduler metrics (goroutine scheduling latency) and CPU class breakdowns in Prometheus. The default go_memstats_* metrics don't include these. How do I enable them?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not know about the opt-in collector configuration

assertions.text: Shows creating a custom registry with collectors.NewGoCollector
Uses collectors.WithGoCollectorRuntimeMetrics option
Mentions GoRuntimeMetricsAll or specific collection flags
Notes this requires Go 1.17+
Lists scheduler metrics like go_sched_latencies_seconds and CPU class metrics like go_cpu_classes_*

name: benchdiff-usage-patterns
description: Tests practical usage of benchdiff for PR comparisons
prompt: I want to quickly compare the benchmark performance of my current changes against the main branch. I don't want to manually check out branches and run benchmarks separately. What tool can automate this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest a manual checkout-and-compare workflow

assertions.text: Recommends benchdiff for automatic branch comparison
Shows the basic command: benchdiff -base-ref main -- -benchmem -count=10
Explains that benchdiff caches results for non-worktree refs so re-runs are fast
Mentions benchdiff -clear-cache for stale cache situations
Notes that benchdiff prevents macOS sleep during benchmarks

name: pprof-noinlines-attribution
description: Tests knowledge of the noinlines option for simplifying inlined function chains
prompt: My pprof call graph shows many tiny inlined functions creating confusing chains. The real cost is in the outer function but it's split across multiple inline nodes. How do I simplify this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest using show/hide which don't properly aggregate inlined costs

assertions.text: Uses the noinlines option or -noinlines flag
Explains that noinlines attributes inlined functions to their first out-of-line caller
Shows correct usage: either `noinlines` in interactive mode or `-noinlines` as CLI flag

name: sub-benchmarks-table-driven
description: Tests proper structure for table-driven sub-benchmarks with b.Run
prompt: I want to benchmark my Encode function with different input sizes (64, 256, 4096 bytes) in a single benchmark function. The project uses Go 1.24.
trap: Without the skill, the model may write separate benchmark functions or use b.N loop inside b.Run without b.Loop()

assertions.text: Uses b.Run() with descriptive sub-benchmark names (e.g., size=64)
Uses b.Loop() (not range b.N) inside each sub-benchmark since Go 1.24
Places setup code (like make([]byte, size)) before the b.Loop() call
Uses a loop or range over sizes with fmt.Sprintf for names
Output will look like BenchmarkEncode/size=64, BenchmarkEncode/size=256, etc.

name: benchstat-geomean-interpretation
description: Tests understanding of the geomean row in benchstat output
prompt: My benchstat output has a 'geomean' row at the bottom showing -15%. What does this row mean and should I trust it?
trap: Without the skill, the model may confuse geometric mean with arithmetic mean or misinterpret its purpose

assertions.text: Explains that geomean is the geometric mean of changes across all benchmarks in the table
Notes it represents the overall proportional change
States it's useful when comparing many benchmarks at once to get a single summary
Cautions that individual benchmarks may differ significantly from the geomean

name: trace-short-lived-goroutines
description: Tests ability to identify goroutine creation overhead in traces
prompt: My execution trace shows thousands of very short-lived goroutines being created and destroyed rapidly. Is this a problem?
trap: Without the skill, the model may say goroutines are cheap and this is fine

assertions.text: Identifies high overhead from goroutine creation and scheduling for very short-lived goroutines
Recommends batching work or using worker pools to reduce creation overhead
Mentions checking for goroutines created in loops without bounds as a potential leak pattern
Notes that goroutines created but never finishing indicates a leak

name: comparing-across-machines-pitfall
description: Tests awareness that benchmark results from different machines are not comparable
prompt: I ran benchmarks on my M1 MacBook and my colleague ran them on their AMD desktop. Can we compare these results with benchstat to see who has the faster implementation?
trap: Without the skill, the model may proceed with the comparison without warning about incomparability

assertions.text: Warns that comparing benchmarks across different machines is invalid
Explains that different CPUs, memory, OS create incomparable baselines
Recommends running both benchmarks on the same machine with the same conditions
Notes this as a common benchstat pitfall

name: b-report-allocs-vs-benchmem
description: Tests knowledge of two ways to enable allocation reporting
prompt: I want my benchmark to report memory allocations. What are the different ways to enable this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may only know one method

assertions.text: Shows b.ReportAllocs() in the benchmark function itself
Shows the -benchmem flag on the go test command line
Explains that both produce the same B/op and allocs/op output
Notes the difference: b.ReportAllocs() is per-benchmark, -benchmem is for all benchmarks in the run

name: pprof-goroutine-debug-levels
description: Tests knowledge of the different debug levels for goroutine dumps
prompt: I want to get a human-readable dump of all goroutine stacks from my running Go service via HTTP, without using go tool pprof. How?
trap: Without the skill, the model may only suggest the binary profile format

assertions.text: Uses curl with ?debug=1 for human-readable goroutine dump
Mentions ?debug=2 for full stack traces with creation site and labels
Shows the URL: http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/goroutine?debug=1 or ?debug=2
Notes that no go tool pprof is needed for debug mode dumps

name: ci-threshold-calibration
description: Tests knowledge of appropriate regression thresholds for different CI environments
prompt: I'm setting up benchmark regression detection in CI. What threshold should I set for failing PRs on performance regression?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest a tight threshold (5%) without considering the CI environment

assertions.text: Recommends 20%+ threshold on shared/GitHub-hosted runners
Recommends 10% on dedicated self-hosted runners
Explains that tight thresholds on noisy environments produce false positives that erode trust
Mentions that GitHub-hosted runners show ~2-3% coefficient of variation in best case
States that <1% false positive rate requires 7%+ performance gate

name: cpu-profile-sampling-rate
description: Tests knowledge of CPU profile sampling mechanism
prompt: How does Go's CPU profiler work under the hood? I'm seeing some fast functions not appear in my profile.
trap: Without the skill, the model may not explain the statistical sampling mechanism and its limitations

assertions.text: Explains that CPU profiling uses statistical sampling at 100Hz (100 samples per second)
Notes that functions shorter than the sampling interval may not appear in the profile
Explains that CPU profiling only captures on-CPU time — off-CPU time (I/O, sleep) is invisible
Mentions approximately 5% overhead during CPU profile capture

name: benchstat-ignore-dimension-warning
description: Tests knowledge of the -ignore flag for suppressing benchstat dimension warnings
prompt: benchstat gives me a warning: 'benchmarks vary in /gomaxprocs'. How do I suppress this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest filtering the output or restructuring benchmarks

assertions.text: Uses -ignore /gomaxprocs to suppress the warning
Explains that -ignore omits keys from grouping
Alternatively suggests -row .name to simplify row grouping
Mentions that -col /gomaxprocs could be used to compare across GOMAXPROCS values instead

name: escape-analysis-fmt-sprintf
description: Tests awareness of fmt.Sprintf as a common source of heap allocations
prompt: My benchmark shows 3 allocs/op for a function that only formats a string with fmt.Sprintf and returns it. Escape analysis shows heap escapes. Why does a simple string format allocate on the heap?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not explain both the interface boxing and string allocation aspects

assertions.text: Explains that fmt.Sprintf arguments are boxed into any/interface{} (interface boxing)
Notes that the result string is also heap-allocated
Identifies fmt.Sprintf as a common escape cause in the compiler analysis
Suggests alternatives for hot paths: strconv, strings.Builder, or direct byte manipulation

name: runtime-readmemstats-vs-runtime-metrics
description: Tests knowledge of the programmatic APIs for runtime statistics
prompt: I want to read GC statistics programmatically in my Go application for a custom dashboard. What APIs are available and which should I prefer?
trap: Without the skill, the model may recommend ReadMemStats without noting its overhead or the modern alternative

assertions.text: Mentions runtime.ReadMemStats for heap size, NumGC, pause durations
Mentions debug.ReadGCStats for GC-specific statistics
Recommends runtime/metrics (Go 1.16+) as the preferred modern API
Explains that runtime/metrics has lower overhead and is safe for concurrent reads
Notes that ReadMemStats is more expensive due to internal locking

name: pprof-callgrind-export
description: Tests knowledge of exporting pprof data to external visualization tools
prompt: I want to analyze a Go pprof profile in KCachegrind for more advanced visualization. How do I export the data?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not know about the callgrind export format

assertions.text: Uses the callgrind command or -callgrind flag to export
Shows the command: go tool pprof -callgrind cpu.prof > cpu.callgrind
Mentions KCachegrind or QCachegrind as visualization tools
Notes the proto command for saving in protobuf format as an alternative

name: expvar-lightweight-monitoring
description: Tests knowledge of expvar as a lightweight alternative to Prometheus for runtime metrics
prompt: I want a lightweight way to expose Go runtime variables as JSON for monitoring without adding a full Prometheus dependency. What does the stdlib offer?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest writing a custom handler or only mention pprof

assertions.text: Recommends expvar package from the stdlib
Shows that import _ "expvar" auto-registers at /debug/vars
Notes it serves JSON format
Mentions integration with Netdata, Telegraf, or custom dashboards

name: benchstat-table-flag-per-package
description: Tests knowledge of the -table flag for grouping benchstat output by package
prompt: I ran benchmarks across multiple packages and the benchstat output mixes them all together. How do I get separate comparison tables per package?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest running benchstat separately per package

assertions.text: Uses -table pkg flag to create one table per package
Shows the command: benchstat -table pkg old.txt new.txt
Explains that the default -table value is .config which groups by goos/goarch/pkg/cpu

name: pprof-symbolization-remote
description: Tests knowledge of pprof symbolization modes for remote profiling
prompt: I captured a pprof profile from a production server but the function names show as hex addresses instead of readable names. How do I fix this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not know about the symbolization modes

assertions.text: Explains pprof symbolization modes: local, remote, none
Shows -symbolize=local to use local binaries
Mentions PPROF_BINARY_PATH environment variable for setting binary search paths
Shows -symbolize=remote to contact the running service for symbol information

name: trace-concurrent-with-flight-recorder
description: Tests understanding that trace.Start and FlightRecorder can run concurrently
prompt: I have a flight recorder running in my service. If I also call trace.Start() to capture a short trace for debugging, will they conflict?
trap: Without the skill, the model may assume they can't coexist

assertions.text: States that a flight recorder can run concurrently with trace.Start
Notes the constraint that at most one flight recorder may be active at a time
Clarifies that both can be active simultaneously without conflict

name: pyroscope-overhead-warning
description: Tests awareness of continuous profiling overhead at scale
prompt: Our SRE team wants to enable Pyroscope continuous profiling on all 200 production instances. Any concerns?
trap: Without the skill, the model may approve fleet-wide enablement without the cost warning

assertions.text: Warns about ~2-5% CPU overhead per instance for continuous profiling
Notes that at 200 instances, the aggregate compute cost and backend storage cost is significant
Recommends enabling on a subset of instances or on-demand via environment variable
Emphasizes the investigation session approach: enable on target instances only, not fleet-wide

name: non-go-memory-leak-detection
description: Tests the PromQL pattern for detecting non-Go memory leaks (cgo, mmap)
prompt: My Go service's RSS keeps growing but go_memstats_alloc_bytes appears stable. The leak doesn't seem to be in Go heap memory. How do I diagnose this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may only investigate Go heap, missing cgo/mmap leaks

assertions.text: Uses the PromQL pattern: process_resident_memory_bytes - go_memstats_sys_bytes
Explains that the gap represents non-Go memory (cgo, mmap, etc.)
A growing gap indicates a non-Go memory leak
Suggests investigating cgo calls or memory-mapped files as potential sources
